problems.title: Two Sum
problems.description:
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return the **0-based** indices of the two distinct elements whose values sum to `target`. You may assume exactly one valid answer exists, and you cannot use the same element twice.

This is a classic hash-map / two-pointer building block seen in most interview loops.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: integer `n` — length of the array.  
Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.  
Line 3: integer `target`.
problems.outputFormat: Two space-separated integers `i j` — the indices, where `i < j`.
hints: A brute O(n^2) pair scan is enough for the smallest n, but a hash map of value → index gives O(n).
When scanning left to right, for each `x` check if `target - x` was seen before.
Careful: return indices, not values; the pair must be distinct indices even if values repeat.

starterCode.python:
import sys
data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
n = data[0]
nums = data[1:1 + n]
target = data[1 + n]
# print two indices (space separated)

problems.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane)
problems.description: Given an integer array `nums`, return the **maximum possible sum** of a contiguous subarray. The subarray can be a single element. An empty subarray is **not** allowed (the array is non-empty in all tests).
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` — number of elements.  
Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.
problems.outputFormat: A single integer — the maximum subarray sum.
hints: Let `dp[i]` = best sum ending at i; or keep running `cur` and `ans`.
Reset a negative running sum to zero is wrong — at least one element: compare `cur + x` vs `x`.
Kadane: `ans = max(ans, cur)` each step where `cur = max(x, cur + x)`.

starterCode.python:
import sys
d = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
n, nums = d[0], d[1:1 + d[0]]

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.description: You are given `n` closed intervals `[start, end]`. **Merge** all overlapping intervals and print the result in **sorted order** by start. Two intervals `a` and `b` overlap if they share at least one point (touching at an endpoint counts as overlap, e.g. [1,4] and [4,5] merge to [1,5]).
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` — number of intervals.  
Next `n` lines: two integers `start end` per line.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `m` — number of merged intervals.  
Next `m` lines: `start end` for each merged interval, sorted by `start`.
hints: Sort intervals by `start` (and `end` for ties).
Sweep: extend current or push new merged interval when `next.start > current.end`.
If `next.start <= current.end`, merge: `end = max(current.end, next.end)`.

starterCode.javascript: const fs = require('fs'); const q = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\n/); const n = +q[0];
starterCode.python:
import sys
lines = [ln.strip() for ln in sys.stdin if ln.strip()]

problems.title: Rotate Array to the Right
problems.description: Given an integer array and a non-negative integer `k`, **rotate** the array to the right by `k` steps. The last element wraps to the front. `k` may be larger than the array length — `k` is effectively `k % n` when `n > 0`.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` and `k` — array length and rotation steps.  
Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.
problems.outputFormat: One line: the rotated array, space-separated.
hints: Reversing three segments can rotate in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
For `k` steps right: reverse all, then reverse first `k`, then the rest (after `k = k % n`).
A copy array `out[(i + k) % n] = nums[i]` is the simplest to code.

starterCode.javascript:
const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number);
const n = a[0], k = a[1], nums = a.slice(2, 2 + n);
starterCode.python:
import sys
d = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
n, k = d[0], d[1]
nums = d[2:2 + n]
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n,k; cin>>n>>k; return 0;}

problems.title: Valid Anagram
problems.description: Given two strings `s` and `t`, print `true` if `t` is an anagram of `s` (same multiset of characters, same length), and `false` otherwise. Anagrams ignore order; only character counts must match. Strings contain only **lowercase English letters** in all tests.
hints: If lengths differ, return false immediately.
Sort both strings and compare, or use a 26-counter array.
Unicode is out of scope — lowercase a–z only.

starterCode.javascript:
const lines = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split('\n');
const s = lines[0], t = lines[1];
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){string s,t; cin>>s>>t; return 0;}

problems.title: Longest Common Prefix
problems.description: Given `n` strings, return the **longest common prefix** shared by all of them. If the empty string is the only common prefix, print a blank line, or a single line with an empty result — use an **empty line** (zero characters) for no common prefix. For this judge, the output is **one line** with the prefix, which may be empty.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` — number of strings.  
Next `n` lines: each a non-empty string of lowercase letters.
problems.outputFormat: One line: the longest common prefix (may be empty).
hints: Use the first string as candidate, shrink while others mismatch.

Or sort lexicographically and compare first and last — only works for "prefix of all" if you also verify; sorting trick: LCP of min and max string in sorted order is a valid approach for *common* prefix? For all strings, compare min to max is insufficient alone — for **all** you still need the scan.
Simplest: horizontal scan char by char on first string and stop on any mismatch in any other string.

starterCode.javascript:
const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split('\n');
const n = +a[0]; const strs = a.slice(1, 1 + n);
starterCode.python:
import sys
raw = [ln.rstrip('\n') for ln in sys.stdin.read().splitlines()]
n = int(raw[0].strip())
strs = [raw[1 + i] for i in range(n)]
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Is Palindrome (Recursive check)
problems.description: Given a lowercase string `s`, print `true` if it reads the same forward and backward, else `false`. In your solution, you **must** use **recursion** to decide: implement a helper `isPal(l, r)` (or language equivalent) and base cases when `l >= r` or `s[l] != s[r]`. (The grader only checks I/O, but the statement matches interview expectations.)
problems.inputFormat: A single line with string `s` (lowercase a–z).
hints: Recursion: compare s[l] and s[r], recurse on (l+1, r-1) until l >= r.
Length 0 or 1 is palindrome: true if l >= r after checks.
Iterative two-pointer also works in practice; follow spec for course/interview use.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){string s; cin>>s; return 0;}

problems.title: Climbing Stairs
problems.description: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Count how many distinct ways to reach the top of a staircase of `n` steps (order matters). `n` is a positive integer. Answer fits in 64-bit signed integer for all tests (n ≤ 45 in constraints).
hints: Ways(n) = Ways(n-1) + Ways(n-2) with base cases 1 and 2 steps.
Fibonacci shift: f(1)=1, f(2)=2, or f(0)=1,f(1)=1 depending on indexing.
Use DP array or two variables rolling.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){long long n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: House Robber
problems.description: You are a robber in a line of `n` houses. Each house has a non-negative amount of money. You cannot rob two **adjacent** houses. Return the **maximum** money you can rob.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` — number of houses.  
Line 2: `n` non-negative integers — money in each house.
hints: At house i, either skip it (take best up to i-1) or rob it (add money[i] to best up to i-2).
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], money[i] + dp[i-2]).
Constant memory: (prev, cur) two variables.

starterCode.javascript: const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number); const n=a[0], nums=a.slice(1);
starterCode.python:
d=list(map(int, open(0).read().split()))
n, nums = d[0], d[1:1+d[0]]
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Unique Paths in a Grid
problems.description: A robot starts at the top-left cell of an `m × n` grid and may only move **right** or **down** to the bottom-right cell. Return the number of distinct paths. Use modular arithmetic: answer fits in 64-bit integer for m,n ≤ 20.
problems.inputFormat: A single line with two integers `m` and `n`.
hints: dp[i][j] = paths to (i,j) = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] with first row/column 1s.
Combinatorics: C(m+n-2, m-1) for unobstructed full grid (same for all cells open).
Base: 1x1 grid has 1 path.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int m,n; cin>>m>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence Length
problems.description: Given an array of `n` integers, return the **length** of a longest **strictly** increasing subsequence (not necessarily contiguous).
hints: Classic O(n^2): dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]).
O(n log n): keep smallest tail for each length in a list.
Strictly > means no duplicates in one increasing chain.

starterCode.javascript: const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number); const n=a[0], nums=a.slice(1);
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Number of Islands
problems.description: A binary matrix represents water `0` and land `1`. An **island** is 4-directionally connected land. Count the number of islands. The grid is rectangular — all rows have the same length.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `r` and `c` — rows and columns.  
Next `r` lines: each has `c` characters `0` or `1` with **no** spaces (e.g. `1100`).
hints: Flood fill from each unvisited 1, mark 0s as visited (or a visited set).
DFS stack or BFS queue for connected components in implicit graph.
8-direction is **not** used — only up/down/left/right.

starterCode.python: lines = [x.strip() for x in __import__('sys').stdin if True]

problems.title: Course Schedule (Possible)
problems.description: There are `numCourses` labeled `0 .. numCourses-1`. A prerequisite pair `[a, b]` means: **to take course `a`, you must first complete course `b`**. Determine if you can finish all courses (no cycle in the prerequisite graph with edges `b → a`). Print `true` or `false`.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `numCourses` and `m` — number of courses and prerequisite rules.  
Next `m` lines: `a b` (take `a` after `b`, i.e. edge from `b` to `a`).
hints: Build adjacency: b → a. In-degrees count prerequisites for each course.
Kahn: repeatedly remove zero in-degree; if you process all courses, true.
Cycle exists iff not all nodes are processed.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n,m; return 0;}

problems.title: Binary Tree Maximum Depth (Level-order)
problems.description: The input is a **level-order** serialization of a binary tree: a single line of tokens separated by spaces. A node value is a signed integer, or the token `null` (lowercase) for a missing child. The tree is built in the usual array representation (e.g. LeetCode style). **Maximum depth** is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root to a **leaf** (a node with no children). A single-node tree has depth 1.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: level-order list, space-separated, using `null` for missing nodes. Example: `1 2 2 3 3` describes a small tree; build with a queue. Root is the first token; `null` consumes a slot but has no value.
problems.constraints: Number of values (including `null` tokens) between 1 and 100, not all `null`
hints: Simulate the serial array to build nodes with a queue, or use parent pointers.
Depth = max of left depth and right depth plus 1, defined recursively; leaf depth 1 in this problem (single node tree depth 1).
BFS: track level count, increment when finishing a BFS level.

problems.title: Fibonacci Number
problems.description: Compute `F(n)` the **n-th Fibonacci number** with: `F(0) = 0`, `F(1) = 1`, and `F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)` for `n ≥ 2`.
problems.inputFormat: A single line with integer `n` where 0 ≤ n ≤ 40.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ n ≤ 40, result fits 32-bit signed (actually up to 40 fits).
hints: Iterative: a,b = 0,1 and loop n times for shift.
Recursion is exponential — prefer memo or loop.
F(0) and F(1) are base.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.description: An array is sorted in ascending order, then **rotated** at an unknown pivot. All elements are **distinct** integers. Given `n`, the `n` elements in order, and a `target`, return the **0-based index** of `target`, or **-1** if not present. You should aim for O(log n) in interviews; any correct method is accepted here.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: `n` — size.  
Line 2: `n` distinct integers, rotated sorted order.  
Line 3: `target`.
problems.outputFormat: A single integer — index of `target`, or -1.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 5000, |values| and |target| ≤ 10^9, all distinct in the array
hints: One half of any range is always normally sorted; compare with endpoints to know which side to binary search.
Check mid against target, then decide left or right half.
Edge: single element array — compare to target.

starterCode.javascript: const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number); const n=a[0], nums=a.slice(1,1+n), t=a[1+n];
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}
